item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you can't tell me what to do.

item.title: Re: Orifices
item.content:encoded: I agree. It be useful to mention ONLY uncommonly thought of orifices, like tear ducts, urethra, and pores… But naming 'em all is just redundancy.

item.title: Orifices
item.content:encoded:
I wouldn't have thought of this without the intervention of
the above streetcar post,1 but…

bleeding profusely from every bodily orifice, including the nose, tear ducts, anus, skin pores, mouth, urethra, and (in case of females) vagina.

this is basically

bleeding profusely from every bodily orifice, including every bodily orifice.

Do we really need the list of orifices? It doesn't really provide any new information, and part of the premise of SCP documents is that they're supposed to be succinct, right? Understated, clinical, efficient. Note that when I took over on scp-1158, one of my changes was having the list of orifices be just "all available orifices".

(I'd also complain that skin pores aren't orifices.)

Footnotes
1. it was certainly very trolley

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I SAID NOTHING HERE

item.title: Wikiwalk Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
edit: edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Show proposed edit
- hide

To link to another skip about a transfigurating disease that tackle the subject from a slightly different angle, I propose the following edit:

Most hosts do not survive the process due to the heavy demands made on the body.

to

Most hosts do not survive the process due to the heavy demands made on the body.1

Footnotes
1. Due to their similarities as fatal contagions that stimulate the production of excess organs, a possible link to SCP-1801 is under investigation.

item.title: Re: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I do agree that its class should be changed though, given the arguments written by Veeleren in April.

item.title: I Was Thinking....
item.content:encoded: What if this SCP had the ability to infect plants, and other organisms other than humans. I feel like that would make the SCP a little more dangerous and interesting. Oh! And what if a certain species gave it a longer lifespan that another, based on the species’s metabolism and strengths?

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent edit by bartman2 has been reverted, as changing object class is considered a major edit that requires permission from the original author or site staff to be made.

item.title: Classification
item.content:encoded: Have to echo the opinions of those a few years ago. This seems on the level of SCP-008 when it concerns threat level and containment procedures. The Foundation does not seem to struggle keeping it contained nor concerned with it aside from personal usage privileges. In the event of containment breach most carriers would die from blood loss. Simply being contained does not trigger genetic mutation as was the case with Subject D-016-4. And the extensive experiment log indicates that the Foundation has a grasp on the nature of the SCP so they're not fumbling in darkness to handle it. The only reason I can see this remaining Keter class is for posterity sake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On site can also mean "exactly where they are found". Presumably this is the meaning here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Infected personnel are to be terminated on site by security forces wearing standard Mission Oriented Protective Posture (MOPP) anti-biological and anti-chemical equipment."

I feel like it should be "sight" instead of "site" though it could be either way. I have no way of knowing the intention of the author, whether they meant infected personnel are to be killed upon being spotted, or whether they meant infected personnel are to be killed on location, but the second version makes little sense to me. How would infected personnel get off site if no personnel were to be evacuated, and even if they did, would the second variation imply that the infected be brought back to the site before they were killed? It's very unclear, unless I am missing something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want a much more extensive test log

side comment

Theory What if 682 was a being infected by this and he is an example of long term infection

item.title: Re: Downgrade to Euclid?
item.content:encoded: I would say that this doesn't deserve to be Keter, since it's relatively easy to contain it, but I don't think it's safe, either, since it requires regular maintenance in order to make sure it doesn't get free. So, basically, yeah, this should be changed to Euclid.

item.title: Re: Downgrade to Euclid?
item.content:encoded:
The only true danger of this is if a containment breach were to happen, and even then most SCPs become quite deadly and inimical to personnel. The probability of a containment breach, however, is abysmally low. The thing itself is kept in a refrigerated room unless a Level 4/O5 says otherwise, and the conditions for experimentation are incredibly restricted:

Full documentation of experimentation with SCP-016 must be submitted before and after samples and duplicates of SCP-016 may be taken… Only authorized personnel may be permitted to obtain samples of and experiment with SCP-016 under BC-L5 containment conditions.

Interpreting the BC to mean bio-hazard containment, we're talking that everybody not being injected with the thing is wearing at least a hazmat suit, and nobody leaves until there's no instances alive. It's like how facilities around the world keep smallpox on ice just in case, but we never hear of a smallpox outbreak anywhere because of being sufficiently careful.
As for the Safe designation, nah. The specimen may or may not have a degree of sentience, so Euclid is because we aren't 100% certain.

item.title: Re: Downgrade to Euclid?
item.content:encoded: Your arguments could even apply to make it Safe. The problem is the potential disaster if it got out (for example, breaking the Foundation's veil of secrecy), hence Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From what I gathered, the organism seems to not have a hive mind, but have access to the knowledge of the infected individual; the chemical engineer was the one who thought of a chemical solution, and the one who was suspended over a mine and told that their container was going to drop solved that specific problem. Also, the reaction of incubating faster when the host is in the presence of human beings further supports this.

As for your second question, most micro-organisms are completely oblivious to what they do to their host. They exist to reproduce, and they try to do so to their best ability. Basically, this is an anomalous strain of Marburg or Ebola, with the ability to use its host's knowledge to survive and spread for a longer period of time. As for the blood vomiting, that's already there:

hosts have been observed projectile vomiting blood to distances of over five (5) meters.

item.title: Downgrade to Euclid?
item.content:encoded:
I understand why the object is dangerous, but the containment procedures basically are to keep it locked up in a fridge. If, if an outbreak were to happen, then the procedure would be extensive, but not really complex: quarantine, then nuke. The article doesn't imply that an outbreak has occurred previously, or that it will likely occur, so the danger is entirely hypothetical. As well, the reference to the hemorrhagic fever aspect of it makes me think of the recent ebola outbreak; the only reason it spread to professionals is lack of proper procedures and/or idiocy. Decently easy to contain, but public hysteria because of what it does.

TL;DR The danger isn't realistically present and the containment procedures are straightforward. Requesting demotion to Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the implication re: the three different responses to being put into a water-filled room meant to imply that the micro-organism has some form of hive mind? The second response seems to imply that it understood attempting to survive the water by adapting to it would be useless, due to the intervention of the Foundation.

Additionally, is it possible that the micro-organism isn't aware that its hosts die via exsanguination? That would explain why an entity that chose to adapt for survival rather than escape upon being exposed to that first scenario hasn't yet attempted to adapt non-lethal blood vomiting or something. It might not even be hostile (assuming it's sentient); just incredibly incompetent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: KaBoom.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel a bit dense here, but what was the third subject attempting?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Look someone with the same profile picture!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject was terminated when the countdown reached zero and the bomb detonated.

I love this. +1

item.title: Criticism
item.content:encoded: Overall, I liked the article. I thought the concept was very interesting, and I admit I giggled like a maniac at the fact that the last test subject was not informed that the timer detonated a bomb. The organism in the chemical engineer using a chemical based reaction to resolve the high-stress situation was very cool to me. I feel like more could be done with that idea, how the organism utilizes knowledge of the person it inhabits to its own advantage.

item.title: Vast in scope
item.content:encoded: I love that this does a great job of presenting a quick snapshot of the Foundation itself. I think this is going to be my go-to "First Article" when I try to introduce people to the site. It's got D-class, containment facilities, an initial containment gone horribly wrong but ultimately successful, ridiculous impossibilities meticulously studied by strict scientific proceedure, an MTF… the whole Foundation is crammed in there.

I loved it. Would also love to see an expanded experiment log. I'll see if I can come up with anything worth sending to you.

Anyway with my upvote you happen to be at +200 here. Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly, so please don't respond to 5 year old posts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been five years

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am surprised at how well this article holds up after all this time. The only change I had to make was remove "DNA Retrovirus" and just have the microorganism directly alter the host's DNA.

This one really does cry out for a longer experiment log, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this article is ancient, but one part bothers me:

the organism will mutate into a DNA retrovirus

That entire sentence just bothers me; especially the term "DNA retrovirus", which sounds terribly unprofessional. I think it would be better to say SCP-016 was a microorganism which emitted custom-tailored retroviruses. It'd at least be more believable, I'd think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: but sometimes a negative reaction is more telling than a positive one. for example if you used the fake adrenaline method and it did nothing but what it usually does but if you showed some one video of how they were going to die they mutated new stronger internal organs that would be infact a very useful test to have done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The core idea of this thing is that it's reactive. I don't think non-specific danger, real or simulated, would give it much to work with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Question, what would happen if you only simulated a stressful situation without actually putting the subject in one? For example pump them full of adrenaline or as previously mentioned tell them that they are going to die, maybe show them video of it happening to other people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This would make an excellent data leakage file.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: could we consider adding a picture?

item.title: Re: Experiment Question
item.content:encoded: 'cos Oxy-Acetylene will burn underwater. But did he have an igniter to start the reaction with? I guess you could have fingernails made of magnesium, iron and carbon, but that would be a stretch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I noticed the "fringe" relation as well, and I also think those writers were borrowing ideas from this site.
Also, real smooth you killed Spiderman.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This remains to be one of the coolest things on the site to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always loved this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't it be interesting to see the mutational reaction of a D-Class that is simply put in a heavily reinforced chamber and told "You have been infected with a virus that will make you bleed to death out of every orifice in five minutes. Escape is impossible."

Really makes me wonder…

item.title: Re: Experiment Question
item.content:encoded: Actually, I think the thing was trying to evolve an oxy-acetylene torch to cut through the steel door of his cell.

item.title: Re: Experiment Question
item.content:encoded: See, that's what I thought, but the part about the subject intaking massive amounts of water threw me off. Thanks.

item.title: Re: Experiment Question
item.content:encoded: Well, acetylene gas is combustible, as is oxygen. I thin you can imagine what would have happened…

item.title: Experiment Question
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's just my lack of chemical knowledge, but I do not understand what third experiment subject was trying to do with the new organ. Could someone explain this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one, especially with the part about adapting organs to survive.

Sounds a little like the virus in a Fringe episode. Especially with the blood exploding part. Only there it was found in an oil pocket, not a seam.
http://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/What_Lies_Below

Hmmm… The more I browse the site, the more I suspect Fringe's writers borrowed some ideas from here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject was terminated when the countdown reached zero and the bomb detonated.

I see what you did there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Should the subject undergo a high-stress situation (such as a life-threatening crisis), the organism will mutate into a DNA retrovirus, changing its survival tactic from rapid reproduction to the rewriting of the host's DNA and stimulation of rapid cell division.

From the tests done, it seems a better description would be that if the subject is trapped that the virus starts rewriting the subject's DNA.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea that its not killing the host intentionally seems to be pretty likely actually based on how it is.

Creepy virus here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Full documentation of experimentation with SCP-016 must be submitted before and after samples and duplicates of SCP-016 may be taken.

The phrasing of this line makes it seem like some words or punctuation may be missing. I'm having trouble parsing it. Of course, I'm recovering from a cold, so I may just have brainfog, but it stumped me enough to make me ask. Apologies ahead of time if it's something simple I'm just missing. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I first though up of this thing way back when, it wasn't really a blood pathogen. It was a microscopic organism, similar to an amoeba (but not like an amoeba clearly) that modified host organisms to better suit their needs and better protect the organism. This is why it was sentient (3), because it wanted to transform into something else, knew what it wanted to do, and did so.

However, I do like clef's rewrite. It's a bit more interesting and realistically in tune with other SCPs. I'll be writing up an incident (hopefully) to better show SCP-016's nature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't they trial it to see if it improves the survivability of agents/ MTF squads?

…

What?

It's incredibly contagious and ends with "near-total exsanguination".

The bodily reconstruction is almost always fatal. And even if it wasn't, it makes carriers more aggressive.

And just imagine this thing mixing with another SCP to become even worse.

Am I missing a joke here? Some kind of meta-commentary on how excessive SCP testing is a bad idea? Because that sounds like an idea so incredibly bad that it actually is pretty funny. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Does the foundation have to contain it? Wouldn't they trial it to see if it improves the survivability of agents/ MTF squads?
Just a thought, although I'm sure the massive hemorrhaging would make it unacceptably dangerous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is excellent; the descriptions of the various mutations/adaptations stand out in particular.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Between the fact that this thing is sentient and the fact that it begins to transform its host in order to help it survive stressful situations, the most hair-raising possibility this SCP raises for me is the idea that the hemorrhagic aspect is unintentional.

Who knows. Maybe, given time, the microorganism will figure out how to infect us without killing us in the process.

On the other hand, perhaps it's trying to keep the host alive in order to allow a wider exposure and more infections. Malicious! Either way, this is a pretty cool one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Worst-case scenario I can think of: the D adapts to be capable of infecting 682 with a modified version of 016. From there, things can only go downhill. Either the modified 016 takes over and changes 682 into something unimaginably bad, or 682 adapts to 016 and… well, you can see where I'm going with this. No matter what side adapts first, it'd be a potential XK-class event. As if 682 wasn't dangerous enough already…

(The reason why I said it was a modified version of 016 is because the main article implies that normal 016 can only affect humans.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels like it's based on the Wild Card virus.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded:
Ok. I think it just struck me because special mention was made of female anatomy when it didn't seem to be strictly necessary.

Bleeding from the vaginal mucosa or cervix, maybe, but just saying vagina is a bit vague for medical terminology. But it's not my article, and hence just a personal observation.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded:
I think the point being made is the distinction from the urethra, which both men and women share, and the vaginal opening itself, with is manifest only in women. This mention is to clarify that bleeding would manifest from both body openings in women. Men, having the signal opening (the urethra), would bleed only from that.

Which makes the experience all the more unsettling for women. In short, women have two holes in front, and will start bleeding from both. The entry clarifies this in a acceptable and wholly correct way.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: That's my point. Men and women have urethras, so there's no point in singling one out over the other in terms of bleeding from the nether regions; you already covered that when you mentioned bleeding urethras.

item.title: Re:
item.content:encoded: Males have urethras too, you know. It's one of the orifices they bleed from.
